{{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Tenpaku faith]] [[Category:Folk religion in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto cults]] [[Category:天白信仰]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Q11]] [[Category:return the see also]] }} [[sn:Tenpaku faith]] [[en:Tenpaku faith]] [[ja:天白信仰]] [[sn:enpaku faith]]

'''Tenpaku Faith''' (天白信仰) is a form of {{ill|folk religion|ja|民間信仰|12=draft}} found primarily in the eastern half of {{ill|Honshū|ja|本州|12=draft}}. Its distribution centers on {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|12=draft}}, extending southward to the Nansei and Shima regions of {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}} and northward to {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県|12=draft}}.
Due to the diverse associations with {{ill|star deities|ja|星神|12=draft}}, [[Suijin|water gods]], and prayers for safe childbirth, various studies and interpretations have been conducted. Since the 1980s, the faith has often been linked to the ancestral deity of the {{ill|Asami clan|ja|麻積氏|12=draft}}, Tenpakuhahajin (天白羽神, also known as {{ill|Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=draft}}), worshipped at the {{ill|Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=draft}} in the ancient province of Ise.
== History of Research == Shun'ichirō Mitoshi noted that the earliest studies on Tenpaku faith date back to the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}. In the linguistic dictionary *Wakun no Shiori* compiled by {{ill|Tanikawa Kotosuga|ja|谷川士清|12=draft}}, there is a reference to "numerous shrines in Ise Province called Tenpaku Daimyōjin, though their exact nature is unclear." Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|12=draft}} in {{ill|Inazawa City|ja|稲沢市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県|12=draft}}), suggesting it was dedicated to {{ill|Venus|ja|金星|12=draft}}, referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.
In 1909, the folklorist {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男|12=draft}} explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to {{ill|wind deities|ja|風神|12=draft}}. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.
From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|12=draft}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. While he leaned toward the theory of Tenpaku being a fusion of Chinese deities Tianyi and Taibai, he remained cautious, awaiting further evidence.
In 1971, Nokiku Imai compiled a detailed map of Tenpaku shrines in *Great Tenpaku Kami*, asserting that the faith predates wet-rice cultivation and reaches back to the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|12=draft}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
Building on Imai's research, Sōmoku Yamada conducted in-depth surveys in five prefectures (Mie, Aichi, Nagano, Shizuoka, and Yamanashi), which he serialized as *Tenpaku Chronicles* in 1977. He proposed that Tenpaku's origins lie in the deity Tenpakuhahajin.

=== Mie Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Mie)|ja|天白川 (三重県)|12=draft}} - A river in {{ill|Yokkaichi|ja|四日市市|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Mie)|ja|天白村 (三重県)|12=draft}} - Formerly part of {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|松阪市|12=draft}}.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Mountain Deity Faith|chapter="Tenpaku New Considerations"|author=Yoshio Horita|year=1980|publisher=Kōshobō}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Tenpaku Faith|author=Shun'ichirō Mitoshi|year=1987|publisher=Mie Prefecture Historical Materials Publishing Association}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Japanese Folk Encyclopedia, Vol. 2|editor=Azio Fukuda|year=2000|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ISBN=4-642-01333-4}}

revision.text:
'''Tenpaku Faith''' (天白信仰) is a form of {{ill|folk religion|ja|民間信仰|12=draft}} found primarily in the eastern half of {{ill|Honshū|ja|本州|12=draft}}. Its distribution centers on {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|12=draft}}, extending southward to the Nansei and Shima regions of {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}} and northward to {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県|12=draft}}.
Due to the diverse associations with {{ill|star deities|ja|星神|12=draft}}, [[Suijin|water gods]], and prayers for safe childbirth, various studies and interpretations have been conducted. Since the 1980s, the faith has often been linked to the ancestral deity of the {{ill|Asami clan|ja|麻積氏|12=draft}}, Tenpakuhahajin (天白羽神, also known as {{ill|Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=draft}}), worshipped at the {{ill|Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=draft}} in the ancient province of Ise.
== History of Research == Shun'ichirō Mitoshi noted that the earliest studies on Tenpaku faith date back to the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}. In the linguistic dictionary *Wakun no Shiori* compiled by {{ill|Tanikawa Kotosuga|ja|谷川士清|12=draft}}, there is a reference to "numerous shrines in Ise Province called Tenpaku Daimyōjin, though their exact nature is unclear." Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|12=draft}} in {{ill|Inazawa City|ja|稲沢市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県|12=draft}}), suggesting it was dedicated to {{ill|Venus|ja|金星|12=draft}}, referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.
In 1909, the folklorist {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男|12=draft}} explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to {{ill|wind deities|ja|風神|12=draft}}. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.
From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|12=draft}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. While he leaned toward the theory of Tenpaku being a fusion of Chinese deities Tianyi and Taibai, he remained cautious, awaiting further evidence.
In 1971, Nokiku Imai compiled a detailed map of Tenpaku shrines in *Great Tenpaku Kami*, asserting that the faith predates wet-rice cultivation and reaches back to the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|12=draft}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
Building on Imai's research, Sōmoku Yamada conducted in-depth surveys in five prefectures (Mie, Aichi, Nagano, Shizuoka, and Yamanashi), which he serialized as *Tenpaku Chronicles* in 1977. He proposed that Tenpaku's origins lie in the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
== Places Associated with Tenpaku == {{Seealso|{{ill|Tenpaku|ja|天白|12=draft}}}} === Aichi Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Aichi)|ja|天白川 (愛知県)|12=draft}} - A river flowing through {{ill|Nisshin|ja|日進市|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nagoya|ja|名古屋市|12=draft}}, named after a shrine to Tenpaku deity in its lower reaches. * {{ill|Tenpaku Ward|ja|天白区|12=draft}} - A ward of Nagoya named after the river. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|ja|天白村 (愛知県)|12=draft}} - Formerly in Nagoya, named after the river. * Tenpaku River - A small river in {{ill|Higashiura Town|ja|東浦町|12=draft}}, also associated with the Tenpaku deity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=張州府志 第6|year=1916|publisher=名古屋史談会}}</ref> * {{ill|Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|ja|天白町 (岡崎市)|12=draft}} - A town in {{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市|12=draft}}, named after the Tenpaku deity. * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Tahara)|ja|天白川 (田原市)|12=draft}} - A river flowing through {{ill|Tahara City|ja|田原市|12=draft}}.
=== Mie Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Mie)|ja|天白川 (三重県)|12=draft}} - A river in {{ill|Yokkaichi|ja|四日市市|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Mie)|ja|天白村 (三重県)|12=draft}} - Formerly part of {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|松阪市|12=draft}}.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Mountain Deity Faith|chapter="Tenpaku New Considerations"|author=Yoshio Horita|year=1980|publisher=Kōshobō}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Tenpaku Faith|author=Shun'ichirō Mitoshi|year=1987|publisher=Mie Prefecture Historical Materials Publishing Association}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Japanese Folk Encyclopedia, Vol. 2|editor=Azio Fukuda|year=2000|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ISBN=4-642-01333-4}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=draft}}

== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Mountain Deity Faith|chapter="Tenpaku New Considerations"|author=Yoshio Horita|year=1980|publisher=Kōshobō}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Tenpaku Faith|author=Shun'ichirō Mitoshi|year=1987|publisher=Mie Prefecture Historical Materials Publishing Association}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Japanese Folk Encyclopedia, Vol. 2|editor=Azio Fukuda|year=2000|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ISBN=4-642-01333-4}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=draft}}
{{Shinto horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Suwa Faith}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Folk religion in Japan]] [[Category:Shinto cults]] [[Category:天白信仰]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Q11]] }} [[sn:Tenpaku faith]] [[en:Tenpaku faith]] [[ja:天白信仰]] [[sn:enpaku faith]]

'''Tenpaku Faith''' (天白信仰) is a form of {{ill|folk religion|ja|民間信仰|12=draft}} found primarily in the eastern half of {{ill|Honshū|ja|本州|12=draft}}. Its distribution centers on {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|ja|長野県|12=draft}} and {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|ja|静岡県|12=draft}}, extending southward to the Nansei and Shima regions of {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県|12=draft}} and northward to {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|ja|岩手県|12=draft}}.
Due to the diverse associations with {{ill|star deities|ja|星神|12=draft}}, [[Suijin|water gods]], and prayers for safe childbirth, various studies and interpretations have been conducted. Since the 1980s, the faith has often been linked to the ancestral deity of the {{ill|Asami clan|ja|麻積氏|12=draft}}, Tenpakuhahajin (天白羽神, also known as {{ill|Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=draft}}), worshipped at the {{ill|Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=draft}} in the ancient province of Ise.
== History of Research == Shun'ichirō Mitoshi noted that the earliest studies on Tenpaku faith date back to the {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代|12=draft}}. In the linguistic dictionary *Wakun no Shiori* compiled by {{ill|Tanikawa Kotosuga|ja|谷川士清|12=draft}}, there is a reference to "numerous shrines in Ise Province called Tenpaku Daimyōjin, though their exact nature is unclear." Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|12=draft}} in {{ill|Inazawa City|ja|稲沢市|12=draft}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|ja|愛知県|12=draft}}), suggesting it was dedicated to {{ill|Venus|ja|金星|12=draft}}, referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.
In 1909, the folklorist {{ill|Yanagita Kunio|ja|柳田國男|12=draft}} explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to {{ill|wind deities|ja|風神|12=draft}}. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.
From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|12=draft}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. While he leaned toward the theory of Tenpaku being a fusion of Chinese deities Tianyi and Taibai, he remained cautious, awaiting further evidence.
In 1971, Nokiku Imai compiled a detailed map of Tenpaku shrines in *Great Tenpaku Kami*, asserting that the faith predates wet-rice cultivation and reaches back to the {{ill|Jōmon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|12=draft}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
Building on Imai's research, Sōmoku Yamada conducted in-depth surveys in five prefectures (Mie, Aichi, Nagano, Shizuoka, and Yamanashi), which he serialized as *Tenpaku Chronicles* in 1977. He proposed that Tenpaku's origins lie in the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
== Places Associated with Tenpaku == {{Seealso|{{ill|Tenpaku|ja|天白|12=draft}}}} === Aichi Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Aichi)|ja|天白川 (愛知県)|12=draft}} - A river flowing through {{ill|Nisshin|ja|日進市|12=draft}} and {{ill|Nagoya|ja|名古屋市|12=draft}}, named after a shrine to Tenpaku deity in its lower reaches. * {{ill|Tenpaku Ward|ja|天白区|12=draft}} - A ward of Nagoya named after the river. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|ja|天白村 (愛知県)|12=draft}} - Formerly in Nagoya, named after the river. * Tenpaku River - A small river in {{ill|Higashiura Town|ja|東浦町|12=draft}}, also associated with the Tenpaku deity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=張州府志 第6|year=1916|publisher=名古屋史談会}}</ref> * {{ill|Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|ja|天白町 (岡崎市)|12=draft}} - A town in {{ill|Okazaki|ja|岡崎市|12=draft}}, named after the Tenpaku deity. * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Tahara)|ja|天白川 (田原市)|12=draft}} - A river flowing through {{ill|Tahara City|ja|田原市|12=draft}}.
=== Mie Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Mie)|ja|天白川 (三重県)|12=draft}} - A river in {{ill|Yokkaichi|ja|四日市市|12=draft}}. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Mie)|ja|天白村 (三重県)|12=draft}} - Formerly part of {{ill|Matsusaka|ja|松阪市|12=draft}}.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Mountain Deity Faith|chapter="Tenpaku New Considerations"|author=Yoshio Horita|year=1980|publisher=Kōshobō}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Tenpaku Faith|author=Shun'ichirō Mitoshi|year=1987|publisher=Mie Prefecture Historical Materials Publishing Association}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Japanese Folk Encyclopedia, Vol. 2|editor=Azio Fukuda|year=2000|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ISBN=4-642-01333-4}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=draft}} * {{ill|Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=draft}}

revision.text:
'''天白信仰'''（てんぱくしんこう）は、[[本州]]のほぼ東半分にみられる[[民間信仰]]である。その分布は[[長野県]]・[[静岡県]]を中心とし、三重県の[[南勢]]・[[志摩地方]]を南限、[[岩手県]]を北限として広がっている。
信仰の対象・内容が星神・水神・安産祈願など多岐にわたることから様々な研究・解釈が行なわれたが、1980年ころから[[伊勢国|伊勢]]土着の[[機殿神社#歴史|麻積氏]]の祖神天白羽神（あめのしらはのかみ、[[長白羽神]]の別名）に起源を求める説が紹介されることが多くなった。
== 研究史 == 三渡俊一郎は天白に関する最古の研究は江戸時代に始まるといい、[[谷川士清]]がまとめた[[国語辞典]]『和訓の栞』での「伊勢国の諸社に天白大明神というもの多し。何神なるかを知るべからず。恐らくは修験宗に天獏あり。是なるべし」と、『張州府志』での大矢村（現[[愛知県]][[稲沢市]]大矢町）の手白明神祠に関する「或日手白作天白、祀太白星、大方天真画相近訛耳」を近い時期の研究として紹介し、後者を「天白を太白星と考えていたようである」としている。[[1862年]] に中村高平は『駿河志料』に「高平按に、[[天一神]]（天一星）、太白神（[[金星|太白星]]）此神二神を祭れるならん」と記し、[[御巫清直]]は『伊勢式内神社検録』で天一と太白の合祭による略称であるとしたという<ref>『天白信仰の研究』187ページ</ref>。
[[明治]]末期の[[1909年]]（[[明治]]41年）、のちに[[民俗学]]の祖と呼ばれる[[柳田國男]]が『山民の生活』で天白を取り上げ、翌[[1910年]]（明治42年）には『石神問答』でも天白に言及した。そこでは古い神であることは疑いがないが過去の天一太白の合成などの説は「憶測の説多し」として退け「[[風神|風の神]]」である可能性を指摘し、のちの研究者に注目されることになる<ref>『日本民俗大辞典 下』「てんぱく」</ref>。[[1926年]]には愛知県[[岡崎市]]で『岡崎市史』が発行され、[[修験道]]から出た風水除の神と推察された。
[[太平洋戦争]]が終結し数年経ったころから天白に関する研究が徐々に進むようになった。柳田國男に師事した堀田吉雄は[[1951年]]から [[1964年]]にかけて『天白神序説』・『大天白考』・『天白新考』を著し、最終的に決め手は何もないとしながら中国由来の天一太白の合成と考えるのが自然とした<ref>『山の神信仰の研究』454ページ上段。ただし456ページ下段では「''果たしてこのように簡単にわりきれるものであるか、どうかなお今後有力な資料の出現をまって考えてみたいと思う''」と結ぶ慎重な姿勢を見せている。</ref>。[[滋賀県]]から[[埼玉県]]の[[郷土史]]研究家の協力を得た今井野菊は[[1971年]]に『大天白神』に各地の天白社の分布をまとめ、天白信仰は[[イネ|水稲農耕]]以前、[[縄文時代]]まで遡るとした。『大天白神』では今井以外の論考も掲載され、愛知県の鈴木和雄は[[風水害]]を受けている場所に限られるとした『岡崎市史』に反論、埼玉県の茂木六郎は[[チベット仏教|ラマ教]]の性神としての「大天白」の信仰を指摘、[[岐阜県]]の田中静夫は「天白波神（天白羽神）を祀った」とした<ref>『天白信仰の研究』188ページ</ref>。
この今井らの研究を受けた[[山田宗睦]]は三重・愛知・長野・静岡・[[山梨県|山梨]]の5県を重点的に調査した。その結果は[[1977年]]に[[中日新聞]]に『天白紀行』と題して連載され、「''天白の起原を天ノ白羽に求める''」とされた<ref>『天白信仰の研究』190ページ</ref>。
== 天白神に関連する地名 == {{Seealso|天白}} === 愛知県 === * [[天白川 (愛知県)|天白川]] - [[日進市]]・名古屋市を主に流れる河川。下流域に天白神が祀られていたことに由来する。 * [[天白区]] - 名古屋市の行政区。天白川に由来する。 * [[天白村 (愛知県)|天白村]] - 現在の名古屋市。天白川に由来する。 * 天白川 - [[東浦町]]を流れる小川の俗称<ref>{{Cite book|和書 |title=張州府志 第6 |year=1916 |publisher=名古屋史談会}}</ref>。 * [[天白町 (岡崎市)|天白町]] - [[岡崎市]]の町名。天白神が祀られていたことに由来する。 * [[天白川 (田原市)|天白川]] - [[田原市]]を流れる河川。 === 三重県 === * [[天白川 (三重県)|天白川]] - [[四日市市]]を流れる河川。 * [[天白村 (三重県)|天白村]] - 現在の[[松阪市]]。
== 参考文献 == * 『山の神信仰の研究』（第3章「天白新考」、堀田吉雄著、1980年光書房発行、1964年の増補改訂版） * 『天白信仰の研究』（三渡俊一郎著、1987年三重県郷土資料刊行会発行、三重県郷土資料叢書） * 『日本民俗大辞典 下』（[[福田アジオ]]ほか編、2000年4月[[吉川弘文館]]発行、ISBN 4-642-01333-4）
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 関連項目 == * [[長白羽神]] * [[機殿神社]]
{{神道 横}} {{shinto-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:てんはくしんこう}} [[Category:日本の民間信仰]] [[Category:信仰]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[d:Q3616095]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Tenpaku Faith''' (天白信仰) is a form of [[:en:Folk religion|folk religion]] found primarily in the eastern half of [[:en:Honshu|Honshū]]. Its distribution centers on [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], extending southward to the Nansei and Shima regions of [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] and northward to [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]].

Due to the diverse associations with {{ill|star deities|ja|星神|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship of heavenly bodies|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[Suijin|water gods]], and prayers for safe childbirth, various studies and interpretations have been conducted. Since the 1980s, the faith has often been linked to the ancestral deity of the {{ill|Asami clan|ja|麻積氏|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asami clan|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Tenpakuhahajin (天白羽神, also known as {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}}), worshipped at the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}} in the ancient province of Ise.

== History of Research ==
Shun'ichirō Mitoshi noted that the earliest studies on Tenpaku faith date back to the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. In the linguistic dictionary *Wakun no Shiori* compiled by [[:en:Tanikawa Kotosuga|Tanikawa Kotosuga]], there is a reference to "numerous shrines in Ise Province called Tenpaku Daimyōjin, though their exact nature is unclear." Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Town|qq=|1=Ōya Town|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20037247}} in [[:en:Inazawa|Inazawa City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]]), suggesting it was dedicated to [[:en:Venus|Venus]], referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.

In 1909, the folklorist [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to [[:en:Fūjin|Wind deities]]. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.

From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Horita|qq=|1=Yoshio Hotta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56348851}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. While he leaned toward the theory of Tenpaku being a fusion of Chinese deities Tianyi and Taibai, he remained cautious, awaiting further evidence.

In 1971, Nokiku Imai compiled a detailed map of Tenpaku shrines in *Great Tenpaku Kami*, asserting that the faith predates wet-rice cultivation and reaches back to the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flood control|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 自然災害|1=natural disaster|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8065}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.

Building on Imai's research, Sōmoku Yamada conducted in-depth surveys in five prefectures (Mie, Aichi, Nagano, Shizuoka, and Yamanashi), which he serialized as *Tenpaku Chronicles* in 1977. He proposed that Tenpaku's origins lie in the deity Tenpakuhahajin.

== Places Associated with Tenpaku ==
{{Seealso|{{ill|Tenpaku|ja|天白|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku|qq=}}}}
=== Aichi Prefecture ===
* {{ill|Tenpaku River (Aichi)|ja|天白川 (愛知県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Aichi)|qq=|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443111}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Nisshin, Aichi|Nisshin]] and [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]], named after a shrine to Tenpaku deity in its lower reaches.
* [[:en:Tenpaku-ku|Tenpaku Ward]] - A ward of Nagoya named after the river.
* {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|ja|天白村 (愛知県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|qq=|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17177017}} - Formerly in Nagoya, named after the river.
* Tenpaku River - A small river in [[:en:Higashiura|Higashiura Town]], also associated with the Tenpaku deity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=張州府志 第6|year=1916|publisher=名古屋史談会}}</ref>
* {{ill|Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|ja|天白町 (岡崎市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|qq=|1=Tenpaku-chō, Okazaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17218861}} - A town in [[:en:Okazaki, Aichi|Okazaki]], named after the Tenpaku deity.
* {{ill|Tenpaku River (Tahara)|ja|天白川 (田原市)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Tahara)|qq=|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132044415}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Tahara, Aichi|Tahara City]].

=== Mie Prefecture ===
* {{ill|Tenpaku River (Mie)|ja|天白川 (三重県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Mie)|qq=|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17174431}} - A river in [[:en:Yokkaichi|Yokkaichi]].
* {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Mie)|ja|天白村 (三重県)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Mie)|qq=|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175728}} - Formerly part of [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q3616095]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Tenpaku Faith''' (天白信仰) is a form of [[:en:Folk religion|folk religion]] found primarily in the eastern half of [[:en:Honshu|Honshū]]. Its distribution centers on [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], extending southward to the Nansei and Shima regions of [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] and northward to [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]].

Due to the diverse associations with {{ill|star deities|ja|星神|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship of heavenly bodies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[Suijin|water gods]], and prayers for safe childbirth, various studies and interpretations have been conducted. Since the 1980s, the faith has often been linked to the ancestral deity of the {{ill|Asami clan|ja|麻積氏|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asami clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Tenpakuhahajin (天白羽神, also known as {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}}), worshipped at the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}} in the ancient province of Ise.

== History of Research ==
Shun'ichirō Mitoshi noted that the earliest studies on Tenpaku faith date back to the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. In the linguistic dictionary *Wakun no Shiori* compiled by [[:en:Tanikawa Kotosuga|Tanikawa Kotosuga]], there is a reference to "numerous shrines in Ise Province called Tenpaku Daimyōjin, though their exact nature is unclear." Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Town|1=Ōya Town|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20037247}} in [[:en:Inazawa|Inazawa City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]]), suggesting it was dedicated to [[:en:Venus|Venus]], referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.

In 1909, the folklorist [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to [[:en:Fūjin|Wind deities]]. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.

From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Horita|1=Yoshio Hotta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56348851}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. While he leaned toward the theory of Tenpaku being a fusion of Chinese deities Tianyi and Taibai, he remained cautious, awaiting further evidence.

In 1971, Nokiku Imai compiled a detailed map of Tenpaku shrines in *Great Tenpaku Kami*, asserting that the faith predates wet-rice cultivation and reaches back to the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flood control|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 自然災害|1=natural disaster|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8065}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.

Building on Imai's research, Sōmoku Yamada conducted in-depth surveys in five prefectures (Mie, Aichi, Nagano, Shizuoka, and Yamanashi), which he serialized as *Tenpaku Chronicles* in 1977. He proposed that Tenpaku's origins lie in the deity Tenpakuhahajin.

== Places Associated with Tenpaku ==
{{Seealso|{{ill|Tenpaku|ja|天白|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku|qq=}}}}
=== Aichi Prefecture ===
* {{ill|Tenpaku River (Aichi)|ja|天白川 (愛知県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443111}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Nisshin, Aichi|Nisshin]] and [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]], named after a shrine to Tenpaku deity in its lower reaches.
* [[:en:Tenpaku-ku|Tenpaku Ward]] - A ward of Nagoya named after the river.
* {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|ja|天白村 (愛知県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17177017}} - Formerly in Nagoya, named after the river.
* Tenpaku River - A small river in [[:en:Higashiura|Higashiura Town]], also associated with the Tenpaku deity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=張州府志 第6|year=1916|publisher=名古屋史談会}}</ref>
* {{ill|Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|ja|天白町 (岡崎市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|1=Tenpaku-chō, Okazaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17218861}} - A town in [[:en:Okazaki, Aichi|Okazaki]], named after the Tenpaku deity.
* {{ill|Tenpaku River (Tahara)|ja|天白川 (田原市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Tahara)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132044415}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Tahara, Aichi|Tahara City]].

=== Mie Prefecture ===
* {{ill|Tenpaku River (Mie)|ja|天白川 (三重県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Mie)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17174431}} - A river in [[:en:Yokkaichi|Yokkaichi]].
* {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Mie)|ja|天白村 (三重県)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Mie)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175728}} - Formerly part of [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka]].

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q3616095]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Tenpaku Faith''' (天白信仰) is a form of [[:en:Folk religion|folk religion]] found primarily in the eastern half of [[:en:Honshu|Honshū]]. Its distribution centers on [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], extending southward to the Nansei and Shima regions of [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] and northward to [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]].

Due to the diverse associations with {{ill|star deities|ja|星神|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship of heavenly bodies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[Suijin|water gods]], and prayers for safe childbirth, various studies and interpretations have been conducted. Since the 1980s, the faith has often been linked to the ancestral deity of the {{ill|Asami clan|ja|麻積氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asami clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Tenpakuhahajin (天白羽神, also known as {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}}), worshipped at the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}} in the ancient province of Ise.

== History of Research ==
Shun'ichirō Mitoshi noted that the earliest studies on Tenpaku faith date back to the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. In the linguistic dictionary *Wakun no Shiori* compiled by [[:en:Tanikawa Kotosuga|Tanikawa Kotosuga]], there is a reference to "numerous shrines in Ise Province called Tenpaku Daimyōjin, though their exact nature is unclear." Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Town|1=Ōya Town|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20037247}} in [[:en:Inazawa|Inazawa City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]]), suggesting it was dedicated to [[:en:Venus|Venus]], referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.

In 1909, the folklorist [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to [[:en:Fūjin|Wind deities]]. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.

From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Horita|1=Yoshio Hotta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56348851}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. While he leaned toward the theory of Tenpaku being a fusion of Chinese deities Tianyi and Taibai, he remained cautious, awaiting further evidence.

In 1971, Nokiku Imai compiled a detailed map of Tenpaku shrines in *Great Tenpaku Kami*, asserting that the faith predates wet-rice cultivation and reaches back to the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flood control|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 自然災害|1=natural disaster|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8065}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.

Building on Imai's research, Sōmoku Yamada conducted in-depth surveys in five prefectures (Mie, Aichi, Nagano, Shizuoka, and Yamanashi), which he serialized as *Tenpaku Chronicles* in 1977. He proposed that Tenpaku's origins lie in the deity Tenpakuhahajin.

== Places Associated with Tenpaku ==
{{Seealso|{{ill|Tenpaku|ja|天白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku|qq=}}}}
=== Aichi Prefecture ===
* {{ill|Tenpaku River (Aichi)|ja|天白川 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443111}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Nisshin, Aichi|Nisshin]] and [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]], named after a shrine to Tenpaku deity in its lower reaches.
* [[:en:Tenpaku-ku|Tenpaku Ward]] - A ward of Nagoya named after the river.
* {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|ja|天白村 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17177017}} - Formerly in Nagoya, named after the river.
* Tenpaku River - A small river in [[:en:Higashiura|Higashiura Town]], also associated with the Tenpaku deity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=張州府志 第6|year=1916|publisher=名古屋史談会}}</ref>
* {{ill|Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|ja|天白町 (岡崎市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|1=Tenpaku-chō, Okazaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17218861}} - A town in [[:en:Okazaki, Aichi|Okazaki]], named after the Tenpaku deity.
* {{ill|Tenpaku River (Tahara)|ja|天白川 (田原市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Tahara)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132044415}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Tahara, Aichi|Tahara City]].

=== Mie Prefecture ===
* {{ill|Tenpaku River (Mie)|ja|天白川 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Mie)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17174431}} - A river in [[:en:Yokkaichi|Yokkaichi]].
* {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Mie)|ja|天白村 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Mie)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175728}} - Formerly part of [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka]].

== References ==
* {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Mountain Deity Faith|chapter="Tenpaku New Considerations"|author=Yoshio Horita|year=1980|publisher=Kōshobō}}
* {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Tenpaku Faith|author=Shun'ichirō Mitoshi|year=1987|publisher=Mie Prefecture Historical Materials Publishing Association}}
* {{Cite book|和書|title=Japanese Folk Encyclopedia, Vol. 2|editor=Azio Fukuda|year=2000|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ISBN=4-642-01333-4}}

'''Tenpaku Faith''' (天白信仰) is a form of [[:en:Folk religion|folk religion]] found primarily in the eastern half of [[:en:Honshu|Honshū]]. Its distribution centers on [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], extending southward to the Nansei and Shima regions of [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] and northward to [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]].

Due to the diverse associations with {{ill|star deities|ja|星神|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship of heavenly bodies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[Suijin|water gods]], and prayers for safe childbirth, various studies and interpretations have been conducted. Since the 1980s, the faith has often been linked to the ancestral deity of the {{ill|Asami clan|ja|麻積氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asami clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Tenpakuhahajin (天白羽神, also known as {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}}), worshipped at the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}} in the ancient province of Ise.

== History of Research ==
Shun'ichirō Mitoshi noted that the earliest studies on Tenpaku faith date back to the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. In the linguistic dictionary *Wakun no Shiori* compiled by [[:en:Tanikawa Kotosuga|Tanikawa Kotosuga]], there is a reference to "numerous shrines in Ise Province called Tenpaku Daimyōjin, though their exact nature is unclear." Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Town|1=Ōya Town|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20037247}} in [[:en:Inazawa|Inazawa City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]]), suggesting it was dedicated to [[:en:Venus|Venus]], referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.

In 1909, the folklorist [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to [[:en:Fūjin|Wind deities]]. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.

From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Horita|1=Yoshio Hotta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56348851}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. While he leaned toward the theory of Tenpaku being a fusion of Chinese deities Tianyi and Taibai, he remained cautious, awaiting further evidence.

In 1971, Nokiku Imai compiled a detailed map of Tenpaku shrines in *Great Tenpaku Kami*, asserting that the faith predates wet-rice cultivation and reaches back to the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flood control|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 自然災害|1=natural disaster|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8065}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.

Building on Imai's research, Sōmoku Yamada conducted in-depth surveys in five prefectures (Mie, Aichi, Nagano, Shizuoka, and Yamanashi), which he serialized as *Tenpaku Chronicles* in 1977. He proposed that Tenpaku's origins lie in the deity Tenpakuhahajin.

== Places Associated with Tenpaku ==
{{Seealso|{{ill|Tenpaku|ja|天白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku|qq=}}}}
=== Aichi Prefecture ===
* {{ill|Tenpaku River (Aichi)|ja|天白川 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443111}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Nisshin, Aichi|Nisshin]] and [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]], named after a shrine to Tenpaku deity in its lower reaches.
* [[:en:Tenpaku-ku|Tenpaku Ward]] - A ward of Nagoya named after the river.
* {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|ja|天白村 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17177017}} - Formerly in Nagoya, named after the river.
* Tenpaku River - A small river in [[:en:Higashiura|Higashiura Town]], also associated with the Tenpaku deity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=張州府志 第6|year=1916|publisher=名古屋史談会}}</ref>
* {{ill|Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|ja|天白町 (岡崎市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|1=Tenpaku-chō, Okazaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17218861}} - A town in [[:en:Okazaki, Aichi|Okazaki]], named after the Tenpaku deity.
* {{ill|Tenpaku River (Tahara)|ja|天白川 (田原市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Tahara)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132044415}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Tahara, Aichi|Tahara City]].

=== Mie Prefecture ===
* {{ill|Tenpaku River (Mie)|ja|天白川 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Mie)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17174431}} - A river in [[:en:Yokkaichi|Yokkaichi]].
* {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Mie)|ja|天白村 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Mie)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175728}} - Formerly part of [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka]].

'''Tenpaku Faith''' (天白信仰) is a form of [[:en:Folk religion|folk religion]] found primarily in the eastern half of [[:en:Honshu|Honshū]]. Its distribution centers on [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], extending southward to the Nansei and Shima regions of [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] and northward to [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]].

Due to the diverse associations with {{ill|star deities|ja|星神|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship of heavenly bodies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[Suijin|water gods]], and prayers for safe childbirth, various studies and interpretations have been conducted. Since the 1980s, the faith has often been linked to the ancestral deity of the {{ill|Asami clan|ja|麻積氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asami clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Tenpakuhahajin (天白羽神, also known as {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}}), worshipped at the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}} in the ancient province of Ise.

== History of Research ==
Shun'ichirō Mitoshi noted that the earliest studies on Tenpaku faith date back to the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. In the linguistic dictionary *Wakun no Shiori* compiled by [[:en:Tanikawa Kotosuga|Tanikawa Kotosuga]], there is a reference to "numerous shrines in Ise Province called Tenpaku Daimyōjin, though their exact nature is unclear." Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Town|1=Ōya Town|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20037247}} in [[:en:Inazawa|Inazawa City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]]), suggesting it was dedicated to [[:en:Venus|Venus]], referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.

In 1909, the folklorist [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to [[:en:Fūjin|Wind deities]]. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.

From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Horita|1=Yoshio Hotta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56348851}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. While he leaned toward the theory of Tenpaku being a fusion of Chinese deities Tianyi and Taibai, he remained cautious, awaiting further evidence.

In 1971, Nokiku Imai compiled a detailed map of Tenpaku shrines in *Great Tenpaku Kami*, asserting that the faith predates wet-rice cultivation and reaches back to the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flood control|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 自然災害|1=natural disaster|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8065}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.

Building on Imai's research, Sōmoku Yamada conducted in-depth surveys in five prefectures (Mie, Aichi, Nagano, Shizuoka, and Yamanashi), which he serialized as *Tenpaku Chronicles* in 1977. He proposed that Tenpaku's origins lie in the deity Tenpakuhahajin.

== Places Associated with Tenpaku ==
{{Seealso|{{ill|Tenpaku|ja|天白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku|qq=}}}}
=== Aichi Prefecture ===
* {{ill|Tenpaku River (Aichi)|ja|天白川 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443111}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Nisshin, Aichi|Nisshin]] and [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]], named after a shrine to Tenpaku deity in its lower reaches.
* [[:en:Tenpaku-ku|Tenpaku Ward]] - A ward of Nagoya named after the river.
* {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|ja|天白村 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17177017}} - Formerly in Nagoya, named after the river.
* Tenpaku River - A small river in [[:en:Higashiura|Higashiura Town]], also associated with the Tenpaku deity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=張州府志 第6|year=1916|publisher=名古屋史談会}}</ref>
* {{ill|Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|ja|天白町 (岡崎市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|1=Tenpaku-chō, Okazaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17218861}} - A town in [[:en:Okazaki, Aichi|Okazaki]], named after the Tenpaku deity.
* {{ill|Tenpaku River (Tahara)|ja|天白川 (田原市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Tahara)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132044415}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Tahara, Aichi|Tahara City]].

=== Mie Prefecture ===
* {{ill|Tenpaku River (Mie)|ja|天白川 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Mie)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17174431}} - A river in [[:en:Yokkaichi|Yokkaichi]].
* {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Mie)|ja|天白村 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Mie)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175728}} - Formerly part of [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka]].

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q3616095]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tenpaku Faith''' (天白信仰) is a form of [[:en:Folk religion|folk religion]] found primarily in the eastern half of [[:en:Honshu|Honshū]]. Its distribution centers on [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], extending southward to the Nansei and Shima regions of [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] and northward to [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]].
Due to the diverse associations with {{ill|star deities|ja|星神|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship of heavenly bodies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[Suijin|water gods]], and prayers for safe childbirth, various studies and interpretations have been conducted. Since the 1980s, the faith has often been linked to the ancestral deity of the {{ill|Asami clan|ja|麻積氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asami clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Tenpakuhahajin (天白羽神, also known as {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}}), worshipped at the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}} in the ancient province of Ise.
== History of Research == Shun'ichirō Mitoshi noted that the earliest studies on Tenpaku faith date back to the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. In the linguistic dictionary *Wakun no Shiori* compiled by [[:en:Tanikawa Kotosuga|Tanikawa Kotosuga]], there is a reference to "numerous shrines in Ise Province called Tenpaku Daimyōjin, though their exact nature is unclear." Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Town|1=Ōya Town|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20037247}} in [[:en:Inazawa|Inazawa City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]]), suggesting it was dedicated to [[:en:Venus|Venus]], referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.
In 1909, the folklorist [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to [[:en:Fūjin|Wind deities]]. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.
From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Horita|1=Yoshio Hotta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56348851}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. While he leaned toward the theory of Tenpaku being a fusion of Chinese deities Tianyi and Taibai, he remained cautious, awaiting further evidence.
In 1971, Nokiku Imai compiled a detailed map of Tenpaku shrines in *Great Tenpaku Kami*, asserting that the faith predates wet-rice cultivation and reaches back to the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flood control|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 自然災害|1=natural disaster|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8065}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
Building on Imai's research, Sōmoku Yamada conducted in-depth surveys in five prefectures (Mie, Aichi, Nagano, Shizuoka, and Yamanashi), which he serialized as *Tenpaku Chronicles* in 1977. He proposed that Tenpaku's origins lie in the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
== Places Associated with Tenpaku == {{Seealso|{{ill|Tenpaku|ja|天白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku|qq=}}}} === Aichi Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Aichi)|ja|天白川 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443111}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Nisshin, Aichi|Nisshin]] and [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]], named after a shrine to Tenpaku deity in its lower reaches. * [[:en:Tenpaku-ku|Tenpaku Ward]] - A ward of Nagoya named after the river. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|ja|天白村 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17177017}} - Formerly in Nagoya, named after the river. * Tenpaku River - A small river in [[:en:Higashiura|Higashiura Town]], also associated with the Tenpaku deity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=張州府志 第6|year=1916|publisher=名古屋史談会}}</ref> * {{ill|Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|ja|天白町 (岡崎市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|1=Tenpaku-chō, Okazaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17218861}} - A town in [[:en:Okazaki, Aichi|Okazaki]], named after the Tenpaku deity. * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Tahara)|ja|天白川 (田原市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Tahara)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132044415}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Tahara, Aichi|Tahara City]].
=== Mie Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Mie)|ja|天白川 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Mie)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17174431}} - A river in [[:en:Yokkaichi|Yokkaichi]]. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Mie)|ja|天白村 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Mie)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175728}} - Formerly part of [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka]].
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Mountain Deity Faith|chapter="Tenpaku New Considerations"|author=Yoshio Horita|year=1980|publisher=Kōshobō}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Tenpaku Faith|author=Shun'ichirō Mitoshi|year=1987|publisher=Mie Prefecture Historical Materials Publishing Association}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Japanese Folk Encyclopedia, Vol. 2|editor=Azio Fukuda|year=2000|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ISBN=4-642-01333-4}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}} * {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}}

'''Tenpaku Faith''' (天白信仰) is a form of [[:en:Folk religion|folk religion]] found primarily in the eastern half of [[:en:Honshu|Honshū]]. Its distribution centers on [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], extending southward to the Nansei and Shima regions of [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] and northward to [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]].

Due to the diverse associations with {{ill|star deities|ja|星神|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship of heavenly bodies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[Suijin|water gods]], and prayers for safe childbirth, various studies and interpretations have been conducted. Since the 1980s, the faith has often been linked to the ancestral deity of the {{ill|Asami clan|ja|麻積氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asami clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Tenpakuhahajin (天白羽神, also known as {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}}), worshipped at the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}} in the ancient province of Ise.

== History of Research ==
Shun'ichirō Mitoshi noted that the earliest studies on Tenpaku faith date back to the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. In the linguistic dictionary *Wakun no Shiori* compiled by [[:en:Tanikawa Kotosuga|Tanikawa Kotosuga]], there is a reference to "numerous shrines in Ise Province called Tenpaku Daimyōjin, though their exact nature is unclear." Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Town|1=Ōya Town|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20037247}} in [[:en:Inazawa|Inazawa City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]]), suggesting it was dedicated to [[:en:Venus|Venus]], referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.

In 1909, the folklorist [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to [[:en:Fūjin|Wind deities]]. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.

From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Horita|1=Yoshio Hotta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56348851}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. While he leaned toward the theory of Tenpaku being a fusion of Chinese deities Tianyi and Taibai, he remained cautious, awaiting further evidence.

In 1971, Nokiku Imai compiled a detailed map of Tenpaku shrines in *Great Tenpaku Kami*, asserting that the faith predates wet-rice cultivation and reaches back to the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flood control|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 自然災害|1=natural disaster|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8065}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.

Building on Imai's research, Sōmoku Yamada conducted in-depth surveys in five prefectures (Mie, Aichi, Nagano, Shizuoka, and Yamanashi), which he serialized as *Tenpaku Chronicles* in 1977. He proposed that Tenpaku's origins lie in the deity Tenpakuhahajin.

== Places Associated with Tenpaku ==
{{Seealso|{{ill|Tenpaku|ja|天白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku|qq=}}}}
=== Aichi Prefecture ===

* {{ill|Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|ja|天白町 (岡崎市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|1=Tenpaku-chō, Okazaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17218861}} - A town in [[:en:Okazaki, Aichi|Okazaki]], named after the Tenpaku deity.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]]
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q3616095]]
Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
'''Tenpaku Faith''' (天白信仰) is a form of [[:en:Folk religion|folk religion]] found primarily in the eastern half of [[:en:Honshu|Honshū]]. Its distribution centers on [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], extending southward to the Nansei and Shima regions of [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] and northward to [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]].

Due to the diverse associations with {{ill|star deities|ja|星神|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship of heavenly bodies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[Suijin|water gods]], and prayers for safe childbirth, various studies and interpretations have been conducted. Since the 1980s, the faith has often been linked to the ancestral deity of the {{ill|Asami clan|ja|麻積氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asami clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Tenpakuhahajin (天白羽神, also known as {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}}), worshipped at the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}} in the ancient province of Ise.

== History of Research ==
Shun'ichirō Mitoshi noted that the earliest studies on Tenpaku faith date back to the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. In the linguistic dictionary *Wakun no Shiori* compiled by [[:en:Tanikawa Kotosuga|Tanikawa Kotosuga]], there is a reference to "numerous shrines in Ise Province called Tenpaku Daimyōjin, though their exact nature is unclear." Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Town|1=Ōya Town|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20037247}} in [[:en:Inazawa|Inazawa City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]]), suggesting it was dedicated to [[:en:Venus|Venus]], referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.

In 1909, the folklorist [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to [[:en:Fūjin|Wind deities]]. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.

From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Horita|1=Yoshio Hotta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56348851}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. While he leaned toward the theory of Tenpaku being a fusion of Chinese deities Tianyi and Taibai, he remained cautious, awaiting further evidence.

In 1971, Nokiku Imai compiled a detailed map of Tenpaku shrines in *Great Tenpaku Kami*, asserting that the faith predates wet-rice cultivation and reaches back to the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flood control|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 自然災害|1=natural disaster|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8065}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.

Building on Imai's research, Sōmoku Yamada conducted in-depth surveys in five prefectures (Mie, Aichi, Nagano, Shizuoka, and Yamanashi), which he serialized as *Tenpaku Chronicles* in 1977. He proposed that Tenpaku's origins lie in the deity Tenpakuhahajin.

== Places Associated with Tenpaku ==
{{Seealso|{{ill|Tenpaku|ja|天白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku|qq=}}}}
=== Aichi Prefecture ===
* {{ill|Tenpaku River (Aichi)|ja|天白川 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443111}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Nisshin, Aichi|Nisshin]] and [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]], named after a shrine to Tenpaku deity in its lower reaches.
* [[:en:Tenpaku-ku|Tenpaku Ward]] - A ward of Nagoya named after the river.
* {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|ja|天白村 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17177017}} - Formerly in Nagoya, named after the river.
* Tenpaku River - A small river in [[:en:Higashiura|Higashiura Town]], also associated with the Tenpaku deity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=張州府志 第6|year=1916|publisher=名古屋史談会}}</ref>
* {{ill|Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|ja|天白町 (岡崎市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|1=Tenpaku-chō, Okazaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17218861}} - A town in [[:en:Okazaki, Aichi|Okazaki]], named after the Tenpaku deity.
* {{ill|Tenpaku River (Tahara)|ja|天白川 (田原市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Tahara)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132044415}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Tahara, Aichi|Tahara City]].

=== Mie Prefecture ===
* {{ill|Tenpaku River (Mie)|ja|天白川 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Mie)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17174431}} - A river in [[:en:Yokkaichi|Yokkaichi]].
* {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Mie)|ja|天白村 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Mie)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175728}} - Formerly part of [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka]].

== References ==
* {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Mountain Deity Faith|chapter="Tenpaku New Considerations"|author=Yoshio Horita|year=1980|publisher=Kōshobō}}
* {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Tenpaku Faith|author=Shun'ichirō Mitoshi|year=1987|publisher=Mie Prefecture Historical Materials Publishing Association}}
* {{Cite book|和書|title=Japanese Folk Encyclopedia, Vol. 2|editor=Azio Fukuda|year=2000|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ISBN=4-642-01333-4}}

== Notes ==

* {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[Category:Has wikidata]][[da:d:Q3616095]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tenpaku Faith''' (天白信仰) is a form of [[:en:Folk religion|folk religion]] found primarily in the eastern half of [[:en:Honshu|Honshū]]. Its distribution centers on [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], extending southward to the Nansei and Shima regions of [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] and northward to [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]].
Due to the diverse associations with {{ill|star deities|ja|星神|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship of heavenly bodies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[Suijin|water gods]], and prayers for safe childbirth, various studies and interpretations have been conducted. Since the 1980s, the faith has often been linked to the ancestral deity of the {{ill|Asami clan|ja|麻積氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asami clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Tenpakuhahajin (天白羽神, also known as {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}}), worshipped at the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}} in the ancient province of Ise.
== History of Research == Shun'ichirō Mitoshi noted that the earliest studies on Tenpaku faith date back to the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. In the linguistic dictionary *Wakun no Shiori* compiled by [[:en:Tanikawa Kotosuga|Tanikawa Kotosuga]], there is a reference to "numerous shrines in Ise Province called Tenpaku Daimyōjin, though their exact nature is unclear." Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Town|1=Ōya Town|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20037247}} in [[:en:Inazawa|Inazawa City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]]), suggesting it was dedicated to [[:en:Venus|Venus]], referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.
In 1909, the folklorist [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to [[:en:Fūjin|Wind deities]]. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.
From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Horita|1=Yoshio Hotta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56348851}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. While he leaned toward the theory of Tenpaku being a fusion of Chinese deities Tianyi and Taibai, he remained cautious, awaiting further evidence.
In 1971, Nokiku Imai compiled a detailed map of Tenpaku shrines in *Great Tenpaku Kami*, asserting that the faith predates wet-rice cultivation and reaches back to the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flood control|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 自然災害|1=natural disaster|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8065}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
Building on Imai's research, Sōmoku Yamada conducted in-depth surveys in five prefectures (Mie, Aichi, Nagano, Shizuoka, and Yamanashi), which he serialized as *Tenpaku Chronicles* in 1977. He proposed that Tenpaku's origins lie in the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
== Places Associated with Tenpaku == {{Seealso|{{ill|Tenpaku|ja|天白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku|qq=}}}} === Aichi Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Aichi)|ja|天白川 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443111}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Nisshin, Aichi|Nisshin]] and [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]], named after a shrine to Tenpaku deity in its lower reaches. * [[:en:Tenpaku-ku|Tenpaku Ward]] - A ward of Nagoya named after the river. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|ja|天白村 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17177017}} - Formerly in Nagoya, named after the river. * Tenpaku River - A small river in [[:en:Higashiura|Higashiura Town]], also associated with the Tenpaku deity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=張州府志 第6|year=1916|publisher=名古屋史談会}}</ref> * {{ill|Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|ja|天白町 (岡崎市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|1=Tenpaku-chō, Okazaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17218861}} - A town in [[:en:Okazaki, Aichi|Okazaki]], named after the Tenpaku deity. * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Tahara)|ja|天白川 (田原市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Tahara)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132044415}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Tahara, Aichi|Tahara City]].
=== Mie Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Mie)|ja|天白川 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Mie)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17174431}} - A river in [[:en:Yokkaichi|Yokkaichi]]. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Mie)|ja|天白村 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Mie)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175728}} - Formerly part of [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka]].
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Mountain Deity Faith|chapter="Tenpaku New Considerations"|author=Yoshio Horita|year=1980|publisher=Kōshobō}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Tenpaku Faith|author=Shun'ichirō Mitoshi|year=1987|publisher=Mie Prefecture Historical Materials Publishing Association}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Japanese Folk Encyclopedia, Vol. 2|editor=Azio Fukuda|year=2000|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ISBN=4-642-01333-4}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}} * {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}}
{{Shinto horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Suwa Faith}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3616095]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tenpaku Faith''' (天白信仰) is a form of [[:en:Folk religion|folk religion]] found primarily in the eastern half of [[:en:Honshu|Honshū]]. Its distribution centers on [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], extending southward to the Nansei and Shima regions of [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] and northward to [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]].
Due to the diverse associations with {{ill|star deities|ja|星神|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship of heavenly bodies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[Suijin|water gods]], and prayers for safe childbirth, various studies and interpretations have been conducted. Since the 1980s, the faith has often been linked to the ancestral deity of the {{ill|Asami clan|ja|麻積氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asami clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Tenpakuhahajin (天白羽神, also known as {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}}), worshipped at the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}} in the ancient province of Ise.
== History of Research == Shun'ichirō Mitoshi noted that the earliest studies on Tenpaku faith date back to the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. In the linguistic dictionary *Wakun no Shiori* compiled by [[:en:Tanikawa Kotosuga|Tanikawa Kotosuga]], there is a reference to "numerous shrines in Ise Province called Tenpaku Daimyōjin, though their exact nature is unclear." Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Town|1=Ōya Town|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20037247}} in [[:en:Inazawa|Inazawa City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]]), suggesting it was dedicated to [[:en:Venus|Venus]], referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.
In 1909, the folklorist [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to [[:en:Fūjin|Wind deities]]. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.
From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Horita|1=Yoshio Hotta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56348851}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. While he leaned toward the theory of Tenpaku being a fusion of Chinese deities Tianyi and Taibai, he remained cautious, awaiting further evidence.
In 1971, Nokiku Imai compiled a detailed map of Tenpaku shrines in *Great Tenpaku Kami*, asserting that the faith predates wet-rice cultivation and reaches back to the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flood control|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 自然災害|1=natural disaster|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8065}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
Building on Imai's research, Sōmoku Yamada conducted in-depth surveys in five prefectures (Mie, Aichi, Nagano, Shizuoka, and Yamanashi), which he serialized as *Tenpaku Chronicles* in 1977. He proposed that Tenpaku's origins lie in the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
== Places Associated with Tenpaku == {{Seealso|{{ill|Tenpaku|ja|天白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku|qq=}}}} === Aichi Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Aichi)|ja|天白川 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443111}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Nisshin, Aichi|Nisshin]] and [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]], named after a shrine to Tenpaku deity in its lower reaches. * [[:en:Tenpaku-ku|Tenpaku Ward]] - A ward of Nagoya named after the river. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|ja|天白村 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17177017}} - Formerly in Nagoya, named after the river. * Tenpaku River - A small river in [[:en:Higashiura|Higashiura Town]], also associated with the Tenpaku deity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=張州府志 第6|year=1916|publisher=名古屋史談会}}</ref> * {{ill|Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|ja|天白町 (岡崎市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|1=Tenpaku-chō, Okazaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17218861}} - A town in [[:en:Okazaki, Aichi|Okazaki]], named after the Tenpaku deity. * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Tahara)|ja|天白川 (田原市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Tahara)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132044415}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Tahara, Aichi|Tahara City]].
=== Mie Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Mie)|ja|天白川 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Mie)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17174431}} - A river in [[:en:Yokkaichi|Yokkaichi]]. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Mie)|ja|天白村 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Mie)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175728}} - Formerly part of [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka]].
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Mountain Deity Faith|chapter="Tenpaku New Considerations"|author=Yoshio Horita|year=1980|publisher=Kōshobō}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Tenpaku Faith|author=Shun'ichirō Mitoshi|year=1987|publisher=Mie Prefecture Historical Materials Publishing Association}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Japanese Folk Encyclopedia, Vol. 2|editor=Azio Fukuda|year=2000|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ISBN=4-642-01333-4}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}} * {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}}
{{Shinto horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Suwa Faith}}
