<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3616095]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tenpaku Faith''' (天白信仰) is a form of [[:en:Folk religion|folk religion]] found primarily in the eastern half of [[:en:Honshu|Honshū]]. Its distribution centers on [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]] and [[:en:Shizuoka Prefecture|Shizuoka Prefecture]], extending southward to the Nansei and Shima regions of [[:en:Mie Prefecture|Mie Prefecture]] and northward to [[:en:Iwate Prefecture|Iwate Prefecture]].
Due to the diverse associations with {{ill|star deities|ja|星神|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship of heavenly bodies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[Suijin|water gods]], and prayers for safe childbirth, various studies and interpretations have been conducted. Since the 1980s, the faith has often been linked to the ancestral deity of the {{ill|Asami clan|ja|麻積氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asami clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Tenpakuhahajin (天白羽神, also known as {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}}), worshipped at the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}} in the ancient province of Ise.
== History of Research == Shun'ichirō Mitoshi noted that the earliest studies on Tenpaku faith date back to the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]]. In the linguistic dictionary *Wakun no Shiori* compiled by [[:en:Tanikawa Kotosuga|Tanikawa Kotosuga]], there is a reference to "numerous shrines in Ise Province called Tenpaku Daimyōjin, though their exact nature is unclear." Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Town|1=Ōya Town|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20037247}} in [[:en:Inazawa|Inazawa City]], [[:en:Aichi Prefecture|Aichi Prefecture]]), suggesting it was dedicated to [[:en:Venus|Venus]], referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.
In 1909, the folklorist [[:en:Kunio Yanagita|Yanagita Kunio]] explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to [[:en:Fūjin|Wind deities]]. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.
From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Horita|1=Yoshio Hotta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56348851}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. While he leaned toward the theory of Tenpaku being a fusion of Chinese deities Tianyi and Taibai, he remained cautious, awaiting further evidence.
In 1971, Nokiku Imai compiled a detailed map of Tenpaku shrines in *Great Tenpaku Kami*, asserting that the faith predates wet-rice cultivation and reaches back to the [[:en:Jōmon period|Jōmon period]]. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flood control|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 自然災害|1=natural disaster|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8065}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
Building on Imai's research, Sōmoku Yamada conducted in-depth surveys in five prefectures (Mie, Aichi, Nagano, Shizuoka, and Yamanashi), which he serialized as *Tenpaku Chronicles* in 1977. He proposed that Tenpaku's origins lie in the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
== Places Associated with Tenpaku == {{Seealso|{{ill|Tenpaku|ja|天白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku|qq=}}}} === Aichi Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Aichi)|ja|天白川 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443111}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Nisshin, Aichi|Nisshin]] and [[:en:Nagoya|Nagoya]], named after a shrine to Tenpaku deity in its lower reaches. * [[:en:Tenpaku-ku|Tenpaku Ward]] - A ward of Nagoya named after the river. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|ja|天白村 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17177017}} - Formerly in Nagoya, named after the river. * Tenpaku River - A small river in [[:en:Higashiura|Higashiura Town]], also associated with the Tenpaku deity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=張州府志 第6|year=1916|publisher=名古屋史談会}}</ref> * {{ill|Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|ja|天白町 (岡崎市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|1=Tenpaku-chō, Okazaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17218861}} - A town in [[:en:Okazaki, Aichi|Okazaki]], named after the Tenpaku deity. * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Tahara)|ja|天白川 (田原市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Tahara)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132044415}} - A river flowing through [[:en:Tahara, Aichi|Tahara City]].
=== Mie Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Mie)|ja|天白川 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Mie)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17174431}} - A river in [[:en:Yokkaichi|Yokkaichi]]. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Mie)|ja|天白村 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Mie)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175728}} - Formerly part of [[:en:Matsusaka|Matsusaka]].
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Mountain Deity Faith|chapter="Tenpaku New Considerations"|author=Yoshio Horita|year=1980|publisher=Kōshobō}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Tenpaku Faith|author=Shun'ichirō Mitoshi|year=1987|publisher=Mie Prefecture Historical Materials Publishing Association}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Japanese Folk Encyclopedia, Vol. 2|editor=Azio Fukuda|year=2000|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ISBN=4-642-01333-4}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}} * {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}}
{{Shinto horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Suwa Faith}}

Its distribution centers on {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}} and {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, extending southward to the Nansei and Shima regions of {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}} and northward to {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}.
Due to the diverse associations with {{ill|star deities|ja|星神|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship of heavenly bodies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[Suijin|water gods]], and prayers for safe childbirth, various studies and interpretations have been conducted. Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Town|1=Ōya Town|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20037247}} in {{ill|Inazawa|en|Inazawa|WD=Q389562|lt=Inazawa City}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}), suggesting it was dedicated to {{ill|Venus|en|Venus|WD=Q313|lt=Venus}}, referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.
In 1909, the folklorist {{ill|Kunio Yanagita|en|Kunio Yanagita|WD=Q971687|lt=Yanagita Kunio}} explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to {{ill|Fūjin|en|Fūjin|WD=Q1483957|lt=Wind deities}}. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.
From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Horita|1=Yoshio Hotta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56348851}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flood control|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 自然災害|1=natural disaster|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8065}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
Building on Imai's research, Sōmoku Yamada conducted in-depth surveys in five prefectures (Mie, Aichi, Nagano, Shizuoka, and Yamanashi), which he serialized as *Tenpaku Chronicles* in 1977. He proposed that Tenpaku's origins lie in the deity Tenpakuhahajin.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q3616095]] Wikidata name: [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:55, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Tenpaku Faith''' (天白信仰) is a form of {{ill|Folk religion|en|Folk religion|WD=Q1068640|lt=folk religion}} found primarily in the eastern half of {{ill|Honshu|en|Honshu|WD=Q13989|lt=Honshū}}. Its distribution centers on {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}} and {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, extending southward to the Nansei and Shima regions of {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}} and northward to {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}.
Due to the diverse associations with {{ill|star deities|ja|星神|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship of heavenly bodies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[Suijin|water gods]], and prayers for safe childbirth, various studies and interpretations have been conducted. Since the 1980s, the faith has often been linked to the ancestral deity of the {{ill|Asami clan|ja|麻積氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asami clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Tenpakuhahajin (天白羽神, also known as {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}}), worshipped at the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}} in the ancient province of Ise.
== History of Research == Shun'ichirō Mitoshi noted that the earliest studies on Tenpaku faith date back to the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. In the linguistic dictionary *Wakun no Shiori* compiled by {{ill|Tanikawa Kotosuga|en|Tanikawa Kotosuga|WD=Q6536708|lt=Tanikawa Kotosuga}}, there is a reference to "numerous shrines in Ise Province called Tenpaku Daimyōjin, though their exact nature is unclear." Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Town|1=Ōya Town|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20037247}} in {{ill|Inazawa|en|Inazawa|WD=Q389562|lt=Inazawa City}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}), suggesting it was dedicated to {{ill|Venus|en|Venus|WD=Q313|lt=Venus}}, referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.
In 1909, the folklorist {{ill|Kunio Yanagita|en|Kunio Yanagita|WD=Q971687|lt=Yanagita Kunio}} explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to {{ill|Fūjin|en|Fūjin|WD=Q1483957|lt=Wind deities}}. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.
From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Horita|1=Yoshio Hotta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56348851}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. While he leaned toward the theory of Tenpaku being a fusion of Chinese deities Tianyi and Taibai, he remained cautious, awaiting further evidence.
In 1971, Nokiku Imai compiled a detailed map of Tenpaku shrines in *Great Tenpaku Kami*, asserting that the faith predates wet-rice cultivation and reaches back to the {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period}}. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flood control|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 自然災害|1=natural disaster|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8065}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
Building on Imai's research, Sōmoku Yamada conducted in-depth surveys in five prefectures (Mie, Aichi, Nagano, Shizuoka, and Yamanashi), which he serialized as *Tenpaku Chronicles* in 1977. He proposed that Tenpaku's origins lie in the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
== Places Associated with Tenpaku == {{Seealso|{{ill|Tenpaku|ja|天白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku|qq=}}}} === Aichi Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Aichi)|ja|天白川 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443111}} - A river flowing through {{ill|Nisshin, Aichi|en|Nisshin, Aichi|WD=Q696777|lt=Nisshin}} and {{ill|Nagoya|en|Nagoya|WD=Q11751|lt=Nagoya}}, named after a shrine to Tenpaku deity in its lower reaches. * {{ill|Tenpaku-ku|en|Tenpaku-ku|WD=Q1155245|lt=Tenpaku Ward}} - A ward of Nagoya named after the river. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|ja|天白村 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17177017}} - Formerly in Nagoya, named after the river. * Tenpaku River - A small river in {{ill|Higashiura|en|Higashiura|WD=Q677745|lt=Higashiura Town}}, also associated with the Tenpaku deity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=張州府志 第6|year=1916|publisher=名古屋史談会}}</ref> * {{ill|Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|ja|天白町 (岡崎市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|1=Tenpaku-chō, Okazaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17218861}} - A town in {{ill|Okazaki, Aichi|en|Okazaki, Aichi|WD=Q242783|lt=Okazaki}}, named after the Tenpaku deity. * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Tahara)|ja|天白川 (田原市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Tahara)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132044415}} - A river flowing through {{ill|Tahara, Aichi|en|Tahara, Aichi|WD=Q501891|lt=Tahara City}}.
=== Mie Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Mie)|ja|天白川 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Mie)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17174431}} - A river in {{ill|Yokkaichi|en|Yokkaichi|WD=Q26410|lt=Yokkaichi}}. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Mie)|ja|天白村 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Mie)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175728}} - Formerly part of {{ill|Matsusaka|en|Matsusaka|WD=Q460829|lt=Matsusaka}}.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Mountain Deity Faith|chapter="Tenpaku New Considerations"|author=Yoshio Horita|year=1980|publisher=Kōshobō}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Tenpaku Faith|author=Shun'ichirō Mitoshi|year=1987|publisher=Mie Prefecture Historical Materials Publishing Association}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Japanese Folk Encyclopedia, Vol. 2|editor=Azio Fukuda|year=2000|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ISBN=4-642-01333-4}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}} * {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}}
{{Shinto horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Suwa Faith}}

== Places Associated with Tenpaku == {{Seealso|{{ill|Tenpaku|ja|天白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku|qq=}}}} === Aichi Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Aichi)|ja|天白川 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443111}} - A river flowing through {{ill|Nisshin, Aichi|en|Nisshin, Aichi|WD=Q696777|lt=Nisshin}} and {{ill|Nagoya|en|Nagoya|WD=Q11751|lt=Nagoya}}, named after a shrine to Tenpaku deity in its lower reaches. * {{ill|Tenpaku-ku|en|Tenpaku-ku|WD=Q1155245|lt=Tenpaku Ward}} - A ward of Nagoya named after the river. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|ja|天白村 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17177017}} - Formerly in Nagoya, named after the river. * Tenpaku River - A small river in {{ill|Higashiura|en|Higashiura|WD=Q677745|lt=Higashiura Town}}, also associated with the Tenpaku deity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=張州府志 第6|year=1916|publisher=名古屋史談会}}</ref> * {{ill|Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|ja|天白町 (岡崎市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|1=Tenpaku-chō, Okazaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17218861}} - A town in {{ill|Okazaki, Aichi|en|Okazaki, Aichi|WD=Q242783|lt=Okazaki}}, named after the Tenpaku deity. * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Tahara)|ja|天白川 (田原市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Tahara)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132044415}} - A river flowing through {{ill|Tahara, Aichi|en|Tahara, Aichi|WD=Q501891|lt=Tahara City}}.
=== Mie Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Mie)|ja|天白川 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Mie)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17174431}} - A river in {{ill|Yokkaichi|en|Yokkaichi|WD=Q26410|lt=Yokkaichi}}. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Mie)|ja|天白村 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Mie)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175728}} - Formerly part of {{ill|Matsusaka|en|Matsusaka|WD=Q460829|lt=Matsusaka}}.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:天白信仰]][[zh:天白信仰]]
'''Tenpaku Faith''' (天白信仰) is a form of {{ill|Folk religion|en|Folk religion|WD=Q1068640|lt=folk religion}} found primarily in the eastern half of {{ill|Honshu|en|Honshu|WD=Q13989|lt=Honshū}}. Its distribution centers on {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}} and {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, extending southward to the Nansei and Shima regions of {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture}} and northward to {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture}}.
Due to the diverse associations with {{ill|star deities|ja|星神|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship of heavenly bodies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[Suijin|water gods]], and prayers for safe childbirth, various studies and interpretations have been conducted. Since the 1980s, the faith has often been linked to the ancestral deity of the {{ill|Asami clan|ja|麻積氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asami clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Tenpakuhahajin (天白羽神, also known as {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}}), worshipped at the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}} in the ancient province of Ise.
== History of Research == Shun'ichirō Mitoshi noted that the earliest studies on Tenpaku faith date back to the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}. In the linguistic dictionary *Wakun no Shiori* compiled by {{ill|Tanikawa Kotosuga|en|Tanikawa Kotosuga|WD=Q6536708|lt=Tanikawa Kotosuga}}, there is a reference to "numerous shrines in Ise Province called Tenpaku Daimyōjin, though their exact nature is unclear." Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Town|1=Ōya Town|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20037247}} in {{ill|Inazawa|en|Inazawa|WD=Q389562|lt=Inazawa City}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture}}), suggesting it was dedicated to {{ill|Venus|en|Venus|WD=Q313|lt=Venus}}, referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.
In 1909, the folklorist {{ill|Kunio Yanagita|en|Kunio Yanagita|WD=Q971687|lt=Yanagita Kunio}} explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to {{ill|Fūjin|en|Fūjin|WD=Q1483957|lt=Wind deities}}. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.
From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Horita|1=Yoshio Hotta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56348851}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. While he leaned toward the theory of Tenpaku being a fusion of Chinese deities Tianyi and Taibai, he remained cautious, awaiting further evidence.
In 1971, Nokiku Imai compiled a detailed map of Tenpaku shrines in *Great Tenpaku Kami*, asserting that the faith predates wet-rice cultivation and reaches back to the {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period}}. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flood control|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 自然災害|1=natural disaster|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8065}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
Building on Imai's research, Sōmoku Yamada conducted in-depth surveys in five prefectures (Mie, Aichi, Nagano, Shizuoka, and Yamanashi), which he serialized as *Tenpaku Chronicles* in 1977. He proposed that Tenpaku's origins lie in the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
== Places Associated with Tenpaku == {{Seealso|{{ill|Tenpaku|ja|天白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku|qq=}}}} === Aichi Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Aichi)|ja|天白川 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443111}} - A river flowing through {{ill|Nisshin, Aichi|en|Nisshin, Aichi|WD=Q696777|lt=Nisshin}} and {{ill|Nagoya|en|Nagoya|WD=Q11751|lt=Nagoya}}, named after a shrine to Tenpaku deity in its lower reaches. * {{ill|Tenpaku-ku|en|Tenpaku-ku|WD=Q1155245|lt=Tenpaku Ward}} - A ward of Nagoya named after the river. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|ja|天白村 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17177017}} - Formerly in Nagoya, named after the river. * Tenpaku River - A small river in {{ill|Higashiura|en|Higashiura|WD=Q677745|lt=Higashiura Town}}, also associated with the Tenpaku deity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=張州府志 第6|year=1916|publisher=名古屋史談会}}</ref> * {{ill|Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|ja|天白町 (岡崎市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|1=Tenpaku-chō, Okazaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17218861}} - A town in {{ill|Okazaki, Aichi|en|Okazaki, Aichi|WD=Q242783|lt=Okazaki}}, named after the Tenpaku deity. * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Tahara)|ja|天白川 (田原市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Tahara)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132044415}} - A river flowing through {{ill|Tahara, Aichi|en|Tahara, Aichi|WD=Q501891|lt=Tahara City}}.
=== Mie Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Mie)|ja|天白川 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Mie)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17174431}} - A river in {{ill|Yokkaichi|en|Yokkaichi|WD=Q26410|lt=Yokkaichi}}. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Mie)|ja|天白村 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Mie)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175728}} - Formerly part of {{ill|Matsusaka|en|Matsusaka|WD=Q460829|lt=Matsusaka}}.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Mountain Deity Faith|chapter="Tenpaku New Considerations"|author=Yoshio Horita|year=1980|publisher=Kōshobō}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Tenpaku Faith|author=Shun'ichirō Mitoshi|year=1987|publisher=Mie Prefecture Historical Materials Publishing Association}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Japanese Folk Encyclopedia, Vol. 2|editor=Azio Fukuda|year=2000|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ISBN=4-642-01333-4}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}} * {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}}
{{Shinto horizontal}} {{Shinto-stub}} {{Suwa Faith}}

In the linguistic dictionary *Wakun no Shiori* compiled by {{ill|Tanikawa Kotosuga|en|Tanikawa Kotosuga|WD=Q6536708|lt=Tanikawa Kotosuga|1=Tanikawa Kotosuga}}, there is a reference to "numerous shrines in Ise Province called Tenpaku Daimyōjin, though their exact nature is unclear." Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Town|1=Q20037247|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20037247}} in {{ill|Inazawa|en|Inazawa|WD=Q389562|lt=Inazawa City|1=Inazawa}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}}), suggesting it was dedicated to {{ill|Venus|en|Venus|WD=Q313|lt=Venus|1=Venus}}, referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.
In 1909, the folklorist {{ill|Kunio Yanagita|en|Kunio Yanagita|WD=Q971687|lt=Yanagita Kunio|1=Kunio Yanagita}} explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to {{ill|Fūjin|en|Fūjin|WD=Q1483957|lt=Wind deities|1=Fūjin}}. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.
From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Horita|1=Yoshio Hotta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56348851}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. While he leaned toward the theory of Tenpaku being a fusion of Chinese deities Tianyi and Taibai, he remained cautious, awaiting further evidence.
In 1971, Nokiku Imai compiled a detailed map of Tenpaku shrines in *Great Tenpaku Kami*, asserting that the faith predates wet-rice cultivation and reaches back to the {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period|1=Jōmon period}}. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flood control|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 自然災害|1=Natural disaster|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8065}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
Building on Imai's research, Sōmoku Yamada conducted in-depth surveys in five prefectures (Mie, Aichi, Nagano, Shizuoka, and Yamanashi), which he serialized as *Tenpaku Chronicles* in 1977. He proposed that Tenpaku's origins lie in the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
== Places Associated with Tenpaku == {{Seealso|{{ill|Tenpaku|ja|天白|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku|qq=}}}} === Aichi Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Aichi)|ja|天白川 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11443111}} - A river flowing through {{ill|Nisshin, Aichi|en|Nisshin, Aichi|WD=Q696777|lt=Nisshin|1=Nisshin, Aichi}} and {{ill|Nagoya|en|Nagoya|WD=Q11751|lt=Nagoya|1=Nagoya}}, named after a shrine to Tenpaku deity in its lower reaches. * {{ill|Tenpaku-ku|en|Tenpaku-ku|WD=Q1155245|lt=Tenpaku Ward|1=Tenpaku-ku}} - A ward of Nagoya named after the river. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|ja|天白村 (愛知県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Aichi)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17177017}} - Formerly in Nagoya, named after the river. * Tenpaku River - A small river in {{ill|Higashiura|en|Higashiura|WD=Q677745|lt=Higashiura Town|1=Higashiura}}, also associated with the Tenpaku deity.<ref>{{Cite book|和書|title=張州府志 第6|year=1916|publisher=名古屋史談会}}</ref> * {{ill|Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|ja|天白町 (岡崎市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Town (Okazaki)|1=Tenpaku-chō, Okazaki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17218861}} - A town in {{ill|Okazaki, Aichi|en|Okazaki, Aichi|WD=Q242783|lt=Okazaki|1=Okazaki, Aichi}}, named after the Tenpaku deity. * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Tahara)|ja|天白川 (田原市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Tahara)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q132044415}} - A river flowing through {{ill|Tahara, Aichi|en|Tahara, Aichi|WD=Q501891|lt=Tahara City|1=Tahara, Aichi}}.
=== Mie Prefecture === * {{ill|Tenpaku River (Mie)|ja|天白川 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku River (Mie)|1=Tenpaku River|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17174431}} - A river in {{ill|Yokkaichi|en|Yokkaichi|WD=Q26410|lt=Yokkaichi|1=Yokkaichi}}. * {{ill|Tenpaku Village (Mie)|ja|天白村 (三重県)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenpaku Village (Mie)|1=Tenpaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17175728}} - Formerly part of {{ill|Matsusaka|en|Matsusaka|WD=Q460829|lt=Matsusaka|1=Matsusaka}}.
== References == * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Mountain Deity Faith|chapter="Tenpaku New Considerations"|author=Yoshio Horita|year=1980|publisher=Kōshobō}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Study of Tenpaku Faith|author=Shun'ichirō Mitoshi|year=1987|publisher=Mie Prefecture Historical Materials Publishing Association}} * {{Cite book|和書|title=Japanese Folk Encyclopedia, Vol. 2|editor=Azio Fukuda|year=2000|publisher=Yoshikawa Kōbunkan|ISBN=4-642-01333-4}}
== Notes == {{Reflist}} == See Also == * {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}} * {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}}

'''Tenpaku Faith''' (天白信仰) is a form of {{ill|Folk religion|en|Folk religion|WD=Q1068640|lt=folk religion|1=Folk religion}} found primarily in the eastern half of {{ill|Honshu|en|Honshu|WD=Q13989|lt=Honshū|1=Honshu}}. Its distribution centers on {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}} and {{ill|Shizuoka Prefecture|en|Shizuoka Prefecture|WD=Q131320|lt=Shizuoka Prefecture|1=Shizuoka Prefecture}}, extending southward to the Nansei and Shima regions of {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}} and northward to {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|WD=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}.
Due to the diverse associations with {{ill|star deities|ja|星神|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|1=Worship of heavenly bodies|2=ja|3=星神|lt=Star deities|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/star deities|13=User:Immanuelle/Worship of heavenly bodies|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, [[Suijin|water gods]], and prayers for safe childbirth, various studies and interpretations have been conducted. Since the 1980s, the faith has often been linked to the ancestral deity of the {{ill|Asami clan|ja|麻積氏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asami clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, Tenpakuhahajin (天白羽神, also known as {{ill|Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahano no Kami|lt=Nagashirahahajin|ja|長白羽神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nagashirahahajin|qq=}}), worshipped at the {{ill|Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hatadono Shrines|lt=Hashiro Shrine|ja|機殿神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hashiro Shrine|qq=}} in the ancient province of Ise.
== History of Research == Shun'ichirō Mitoshi noted that the earliest studies on Tenpaku faith date back to the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}. In the linguistic dictionary *Wakun no Shiori* compiled by {{ill|Tanikawa Kotosuga|en|Tanikawa Kotosuga|WD=Q6536708|lt=Tanikawa Kotosuga|1=Tanikawa Kotosuga}}, there is a reference to "numerous shrines in Ise Province called Tenpaku Daimyōjin, though their exact nature is unclear." Similarly, *Chōshū Fushi* mentions the Tenpaku Shrine in Ōya Village (now {{ill|Ōya Town|ja|大矢町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōya Town|1=Q20037247|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20037247}} in {{ill|Inazawa|en|Inazawa|WD=Q389562|lt=Inazawa City|1=Inazawa}}, {{ill|Aichi Prefecture|en|Aichi Prefecture|WD=Q80434|lt=Aichi Prefecture|1=Aichi Prefecture}}), suggesting it was dedicated to {{ill|Venus|en|Venus|WD=Q313|lt=Venus|1=Venus}}, referred to as Taibakusei (太白星). In 1862, Takahira Nakamura theorized in *Suruga Shiryō* that the deity might represent a synthesis of Tianyi (天一) and Taibai (太白) deities.
In 1909, the folklorist {{ill|Kunio Yanagita|en|Kunio Yanagita|WD=Q971687|lt=Yanagita Kunio|1=Kunio Yanagita}} explored Tenpaku in *Life of the Mountain People*. In subsequent works, he dismissed earlier speculative theories, proposing that Tenpaku was likely connected to {{ill|Fūjin|en|Fūjin|WD=Q1483957|lt=Wind deities|1=Fūjin}}. This theory gained significant attention among later scholars.
From the post-World War II period, research on Tenpaku progressed. {{ill|Yoshio Horita|ja|堀田吉雄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yoshio Horita|1=Yoshio Hotta|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q56348851}}, a disciple of Yanagita, published several studies between 1951 and 1964. While he leaned toward the theory of Tenpaku being a fusion of Chinese deities Tianyi and Taibai, he remained cautious, awaiting further evidence.
In 1971, Nokiku Imai compiled a detailed map of Tenpaku shrines in *Great Tenpaku Kami*, asserting that the faith predates wet-rice cultivation and reaches back to the {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jōmon period|1=Jōmon period}}. The publication also included analyses suggesting connections to {{ill|flood control|ja|風水害|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/flood control|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 自然災害|1=Natural disaster|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q8065}}, Tibetan Lamaist sexual symbolism, and the deity Tenpakuhahajin.
